purpose: Signal shift from Exit Check to reflection/connection.
guide: "You read and created bar graphs with scale of 10. You figured out values at the lines AND between the lines. Let's look at some patterns you discovered."

purpose: Students recognize that scales follow a pattern (1 → 2 → 5 → 10) and understand WHY larger scales are useful.
visual:
Four bar graphs side by side showing SAME data (value of 20). All bars reach the SAME physical height. Axis divisions differ:
  - Graph 1: Scale of 1 (axis: 0, 1, 2, 3... 20) — 20 lines
  - Graph 2: Scale of 2 (axis: 0, 2, 4, 6... 20) — 10 lines
  - Graph 3: Scale of 5 (axis: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20) — 4 lines
  - Graph 4: Scale of 10 (axis: 0, 10, 20) — 2 lines
guide: "Look at all four graphs. They show the SAME amount—20. Which scale uses the fewest lines to show the same amount?"
prompt: "Which scale uses the fewest lines to show the same amount?"
on_correct: "Scale of 10 uses the fewest lines. Each line shows more, so you need fewer of them."
remediation_light: "Count the lines on each axis. Which has the fewest?"
connection_guide_states_after_task: "You've learned four scales now: 1, 2, 5, and 10. Each one is useful for different sized numbers. Bigger numbers work better with bigger scales."

purpose: Students recognize that vertical and horizontal graphs show the same data—the orientation changes but the math doesn't.
visual:
Same data shown two ways side-by-side:
  - Left: Vertical bar graph (scale of 10; includes at least one between-lines value at 35)
  - Right: Horizontal bar graph (same data, same scale)
  - Category with value 35 highlighted in both graphs
guide: "Here's the same data shown two ways—vertical and horizontal. Find the category with 35 in BOTH graphs."
student_action: Click on the 35 bar in vertical graph, then in horizontal graph
on_correct: "Same value, same position between the lines—just different directions."
remediation_light: "Find 35 on the axis in each graph. Which bar ends there?"
connection_guide_states_after_task: "The axis works the same way whether bars go up or sideways. The math doesn't change—only the direction."

purpose: Students connect scale of 10 to familiar contexts where counting by 10s is natural.
visual:
Three real-world scenario images:
  - A: Counting money (stacks of 10 dollar bills)
  - B: Measuring height (ruler marked in 10s of centimeters)
  - C: Counting items one by one
guide: "Scale of 10 is useful when numbers are bigger. Where might you see counting by 10s outside of math class?"
prompt: "Which situations would work well with a scale of 10? Select all that apply."
student_action: Multi-select: A and B (both involve counting by 10s)
on_correct: "Money and measuring often use 10s. That's why scale of 10 feels natural for bigger amounts."
connection_guide_states_after_task: "You already know how to count by 10s. Bar graphs use the same skill you use with money and measuring."

purpose: Help students recognize the strategy they used for reading between-the-lines values.
visual: Bar Graphs (Mode 1: Reading). One bar graph with a single bar ending at 45 (between 40–50 lines). Bar highlighted. Ghost gridlines at 5s visible.
guide: "This bar shows 45—between the lines. How did you figure that out during the lesson?"
prompt: "What strategy helped you find values between the lines?"
student_action:
[Multiple choice:
  - "I picked the line closest to the top of the bar"
  - "I added the two numbers on the lines"
  - "I found halfway—that's always 5 more"
  - "I guessed"]
on_correct: "That's the pattern! Half of 10 is 5, so halfway between any two lines is 5 more than the lower line."
connection: (Guide states after task) "Knowing that pattern means you can read ANY bar graph with scale of 10—even values between the lines. That's a powerful strategy."
purpose_2: Specific, growth-oriented affirmation connecting to future learning.
guide_2: "You learned to read values at the lines AND between the lines. You used 'half of 10 is 5' to figure out tricky values."
guide_3: "That pattern-finding will help you when we look at ALL the scales together next time—you'll be able to pick the best one for any data."
voice_note: Specific to what student learned (interpolation, pattern recognition). Not generic praise. Connects to M5 (scale selection).
